critique.summary: The article discusses 'The Secret Agent,' Brazil's Oscar entry for Best International Feature Film, highlighting its cinematography and thematic exploration of Brazil's cultural contradictions during the military dictatorship. It features insights from cinematographer Evgenia Alexandrova on her artistic choices and the film's visual style.
mainPoints: The film is set in 1977 during Brazil's military dictatorship and follows a political dissident.
Cinematographer Evgenia Alexandrova uses vibrant colors to contrast the film's dark themes.
The article details specific filming techniques and challenges faced during production.
critique.truthfulness: The article appears to accurately describe the film's setting, themes, and cinematographic techniques. However, it does not provide external sources or perspectives to verify the claims made about the film's content and production.
considerations: The article lacks citations from other sources or critical reviews of the film.
There is no mention of audience reception or critical analysis of the film's themes.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the film 'The Secret Agent,' with commenters sharing mixed reactions about its cinematography, narrative structure, and emotional impact. Some praise its immersive depiction of 1970s Brazil and strong performances, while others find it meandering or less impactful compared to similar films.

comments.author: beepbooptheory
comments.text:
One thing I noticed is that both this and another incredible film this year, Sirāt, were, at least in part, funded by a grants and state institutions.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sir%C4%81t

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Secret_Agent_(2025_film)

If you haven't seen either, highly recommended. Don't watch Sirat if you're wanting a "good time," but I honestly can't think of the last time a film made me feel the way it did, especially the final minutes of it.

The Secret Agent is maybe as good though. Makes you want to say "they don't make them like this anymore.." It feels like a good long novel; every character, however minor, is rich, full of life, in some way beautiful. It's something about how the past has these pockets of clarity, bookended by loose ends and uncertainty. The mix of myth and anecdote. Pieces of life we can remember, those we can't... Five bags of popcorn.
comments.analysis: This comment provides context about the film's funding and offers a nuanced appreciation of its narrative depth, supported by references to related films.

comments.author: pearlsontheroad
comments.text: Having grown up in Brazil in the 70s, I thought the cinematography of "The Secret Agent" absolutely nailed the aesthetics of that era.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a credible personal perspective on the film's authentic portrayal of the 1970s Brazil, enhancing its credibility.

comments.author: calmkeepai
comments.text: The immersion into the time and place was fantastic, the surreal elements being bold , outlandish, and unexpected were great. The time jump at the end was interesting. a great piece of work that some felt divided over as a general audience but overall memorable and ambitious
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully highlights the film's immersive qualities and stylistic choices, contributing positively to the discussion.

comments.author: zemvpferreira
comments.text: I found it unsatisfying. Such a strong opening let down by a meandering movie with no payoff. Made all the sadder by great moments and performances spread thinly through the 2-something hours. I remember coming out of the movie theatre thinking there was a really enjoyable film buried underneath the crud if they could have had more restraint in the editing room. To each their own I suppose.
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective but reasoned critique about pacing and editing, which is a valid perspective though less universally applicable.

comments.author: neves
comments.text:
I've had the privilege to watch it at São Luis movie theater in Recife. The place is really a character of the movie. The theater was at full capacity. 10% of the public was with a yellow t-shirt that Wagner Moura uses for 10s :-) I forgot how good it was to see movies in a theater. Everybody laughed or clapped together. I've never more seen a movie with so much popular appeal. People seeing their lives and history in the big screen. If it had won an Oscar, maybe São Luiz would exhibit it frequently. Like the Casablanca theater in Morocco that just exhibits Casablanca movie.

Pure cinema.
comments.analysis: Provides an enthusiastic personal experience emphasizing the film's popular appeal and cultural resonance.

comments.author: anderber
comments.text: The Secret Agent was not an easy movie for the average movie watcher. It had an unorthodox ending, graphic violence, and it's in a different language. With that said, it's too bad it wasn't able to come out with any Oscars. I can see why OBAA won quite a few awards.
comments.analysis: Offers a realistic assessment of the film's accessibility and style, explaining possible reasons for its limited awards success.

comments.author: haunter
comments.text: Decent film but to me 'I'm Still Here' (Ainda Estou Aqui) was still a too fresh experience from last year to have  a similar film again from Brazil set in the 70s covering the military dictatorship. I also think that I'm Still Here is a much better film.
comments.analysis: Provides a comparative opinion that may be subjective but is relevant for viewers interested in the genre or theme.

comments.author: basiliobeltran
comments.text: One of the strongest movie start sequences in a while, it immediately sets the vibe.
comments.analysis: Brief but constructive comment praising the film's opening sequence, adding to the overall positive feedback.

comments.author: next_xibalba
comments.text: I watched both The Secret Agent and One Battle After Another (didn’t see any of the other nominees). For me, The Secret Agent was definitely better.
comments.analysis: A subjective but relevant comparison that helps contextualize the film's reception among contemporaries.
